author: @annaplojharova1400
text:
As a fun experiment it is perfect, but otherwise not that much practical, contrary to the expectations it will fail very quickly.
Plastic may be rust proof in wet environment, but it won't be contamination proof (uses to be present in the water) and definitely it will be extremely sensitive to ozone and UV corrosion (mainly relevant for the wind turbines).
In a water you will be better off with a (stainless) steel sliding in a wood bushing, a combination where the water form great lubrication (there are 100 year old turbines using wet hard wood bushing to iron rod bearings working to this day). You just have to make sure it never dries out, that would seize and kill it.
And for wind, classic sealed metal bearing with a reasonable rain cover is nearly impossible to beat...

author: @sfco1299
text:
Perhaps slightly less longevity, but Airsoft BBs are a -super- cheap option for this. £10 used to get you 2000 'hard' ones and they have good manufacturing accuracy too! 5.6mm diameter from recollection, never seen one out of spec when I've been using them for this.
People complain about them absorbing moisture if left unsealed but we know that pain with PLA and PETG anyway :)

author: @joeschmo622
text:
My only concern with printed parts would be the maximum load/speed.
When I was making an RC boat, which wanted to turn into a RC submarine when it would fill with water, I decided to do it right, so the shaft leading to the propeller would be essentially a pair of loose sleeve bearings, but separated by a sealed cylindrical gap that I'd fill with thick grease.  There might be some axial pushing/pulling from accelerating/decelerating, but almost zero side-load, so it worked quite well.
I'm guessing side-load on any bearing would be the killer.

replies.author: @lrmackmcbride7498
replies.text: They make axial bearings specifically designed to be axially loaded.  As well as taper bearings that can handle both axial and lateral load.

author: @William_Hada
text: Great idea! Printable Nylon filament might be a good choice for more durability and toughness.

replies.author: @russellzauner
replies.text: Nylon is hydrophilic, it will change properties in humidity and if you don't keep your filament in a dry environment it will have steam bubbles popping when you try to print with it.  Hard learned experience.

replies.author: @William_Hada
replies.text: @russellzauner Of course nylon is sensitive to moisture and nylon print filament must be kept dry. But once it is printed into a bearing it makes an excellent weather resistant outdoor material. Nylon has been widely used for a long time in bushings and bearings outdoors.  Are you worried about recycling of nylon from bearings back into printing filament?

replies.author: @russellzauner
replies.text: Glass may deform under stressful conditions, although it shouldn't break (see drop demonstrations of large glass balls), but there exist super cheap ceramic bearings, which he mentioned, which can be made to very tight tolerances as well as of engineered materials that might be better in your application.

replies.author: @neilb1619
replies.text:
Hi Hoog, sorry to hear about your problems.
I don't have a 3-d printer either, but there are companies that will 3-d print items for you. A quick Google search turned up several.
I haven't checked prices yet, so cannot advise in that regard, but it might get you going until (like me) you get your own 3-d printer.
The other advantage of commercial printing is the variety of materials offered to print from, including metals...
I hope this helps.
All the best.

author: @xzendon
text: Ok, so I'm thinking that there's some opportunity for improvement here. Are there any cheap and abundant conical bits of metal one could use as contact surfaces within a printed housing? Conical to reduce sizing constraints, that way the balls can be larger or smaller, and you just move the conical slices in or out from the midplane.
